I don't know.  It seems a bit too roundabout to me. Writing the header here, then copying some of them to a dict and then copying them back to a header in the `makeFitsHeader` function.  I'm not sure that's the best way to go.

My thought was that the wcs would just be the regular wcs, and then all the extra header items would be set by `makeFitsHeader`.  But I think I see the logic of your scheme.  Some of the extra keys are intermediate calculations of the wcs construction.  Well, one anyway.  `ORIENTAT` is the `pa_sca` value, which I guess we don't want to have to recalculate.

So given that, I think a simpler approach for both the implementation and the user interface would be the following:
- At the end of this function, after you make `wcs = galsim.GSFitsWCS(header=header)`, just do `wcs.header = header` to save the full header information.
- Then rather than have `galsim.fits.write` look for `image.header`, we could have `wcs.writeToFitsHeader` look for `wcs.header` and copy that over first before doing the class-specific write.  Then the user wouldn't have to do anything at all special to get the right header stuff into the final FITS file.
- We could update the other FitsWCS classes to automatically make this wcs.header field when they read in from a FITS file, so the output files would include any original header info that was in the input file.  (Possibly toggle this behavior with a `save_header` kwarg.)

@kislayabhi When you are just using ```new_cardinality = self.cardinality[::-1]``` you are basically doing a shallow copy. You can find more about about shallow copy and deep copy at https://docs.python.org/2/library/copy.html. So when you change the elements of ```new_cardinality``` then it would change the values of ```self.cardinality``` as ```new_cardinality``` contains the references of the objects present in ```self.cardinality```.

So I would suggest you to do something like this

```python
new_cardinality = np.array([var_card for var_card in self.cardinality[::-1]])
```

This way you are not creating a shallow copy rather you are creating a new array object with recursively adding the copy of the objects in ```self.cardinality``` so changing the elements of ```new_cardinality``` won't have any effect on ```self.cardinality```. 

Hope this makes sense :)

Yes, you are correct that one can leverage scipy.stats to grab the higher order moments of any distribution.  

However, suffice it to say there are apparently a number of subtleties in calculating these features with respect to their physical interpretation.  First, depending on the need, it doesn't seem necessarily best that one should ignore the complex angular part of the fourier spectrum (Ref [1]).  Second, apparently there is some variance in how people implement spectral kurtosis.  Ref [2]'s implementation is slightly different (by an additive factor be a big deal) from scipy's two implementations.  I would have to give some thought to these implementations before feeling comfortable pull requesting them into tsfresh.

Refs:
[1] https://hal.archives-ouvertes.fr/hal-00021302/document
[2] https://de.mathworks.com/matlabcentral/fileexchange/48914-welch-s-estimate-of-the-spectral-kurtosis

But yes, I naïvely think somebody for the time being could just use scipy.stats's implementation and go with it.

I found that I missed that if we store `self.prev_headers` as a list is good for access `block.blockhash(uint blockNumber)` in contract code, we could make `VMState.get_ancestor_hash(block_number)` get block_hash via block_number directly since the `prev_headers` should be chained already:
```python
    def get_ancestor_hash(self, block_number):
        """
        Return the hash of the ancestor with the given block number.
        """
        ancestor_depth = self.block_header.block_number - block_number - 1
        if (ancestor_depth >= MAX_PREV_HEADER_DEPTH or
                ancestor_depth < 0 or
                ancestor_depth >= len(self.prev_headers)):            
            return b''
        header = self.prev_headers[ancestor_depth]
        return header.hash
```
Then we get O(1) for BLOCKHASH opcode.

And since `VMState` has its block properties, we can make `VMState.get_parent_header(block_header)` into `@property parent_header()` for 
 `VMState.validate_block(block)`:
```python
    @property
    def parent_header(self):
        return self.prev_headers[0]
```

But, unfortunately, `VMState.validate_uncle(uncle)` still requires calling `VMState.get_block_header_by_hash(uncle.parent_hash)`:
https://github.com/hwwhww/py-evm/blob/81b4b1ae1c854c97540b63ebd41f196c83a35dad/evm/vm/forks/frontier/vm_state.py#L305

Look on the bright side, `MAX_UNCLE_DEPTH` is 6.

With these changes, I'd vote for accessing via `block_number` directly for sharding clients. But for non-sharding clients, they may call `validate_uncle` more than call BLOCKHASH(many-ancestors-ago).

p.s. EIP 96 would make `BLOCKHASH` very different: https://github.com/ethereum/EIPs/pull/210

Fair point regarding the history. I did test it and got streams from the bbc in vlc. I'll outline my motivations.

**Why inspect the history of the response?**
When you perform auth against [https://account.bbc.com/signin](https://account.bbc.com/signin), if the credentials are invalid, then there are no HTTP redirects. You are presented with an HTML page prompting by a picture of David Attenborough sitting on a beach telling you to log in. So, the quickest and (imho) least complicated way to check if it worked, is if you were redirected at all, eg `len(result_history) == 0`.

FYI, the auth flow should work outside the UK, as I think it's part of the BBC's attempt to sell videos overseas. So you should be able to use the BBC id with the account url above.

**Why not check the redirect url against the response?**
